reasoning.typography: Generic Inter/Inter Display stack with no character—the exact stencil mentioned in the rubric. Zero typographic identity.
reasoning.hierarchy: Error page has clear dominance with headline, but this is boilerplate, not designed hierarchy—works by default, not by intention.
reasoning.distinctiveness: 100% swappable error page. Could be literally any SaaS company. No brand differentiation whatsoever.
reasoning.color: Near-monochrome with restrained black buttons—palette discipline by default of being an error page, not by design ambition.
reasoning.density: Excessive whitespace with centered content floating in void—safe and boring rather than confident. Generic uniform padding around CTAs.
reasoning.voice: Corporate apology template. 'Our team have been alerted' + reload/support CTAs is the universal error stencil. Zero personality or brand voice.

winning_patterns: Clean, uncluttered error state without unnecessary decoration
Restrained color palette keeps focus on message
Clear CTA pairing (primary/secondary) follows basic UX patterns
anti_patterns: Completely generic Inter typography—the AI stencil incarnate
Error message could be copied from any SaaS site verbatim
Wasteland of whitespace with no density or confidence
Zero brand personality—'Something has gone wrong' is the most generic possible headline
Corporate speak ('Our team have been alerted') instead of human voice
Centered floating content is the safest, most timid layout choice
